schools.summary: Gill: having described the land's borders and division, God makes provision for the Levites; though they had no inheritance as a tribe, it was proper they should have cities and houses to dwell in.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses in the plains of Moab
provision for the Levites without a land-portion
the people charged to maintain God's servants

schools.summary: Gill: this is not a bare instruction, much less a request left to their option, but strictly enjoined by the Lord, who had freely given them all they should possess and had a right to all they had.

themes: Command the children of Israel, that they give unto the Levites... cities to dwell in... suburbs
a duty owed, not a favour offered
rendering back a part of what God first gave

schools.summary: Gill: the cities were for them and their families, having no trades nor worldly business to carry on.
Ellicott: 'cattle' denotes oxen and beasts of burden, 'goods' the sheep and goats.

themes: The cities shall they have to dwell in; and the suburbs... for their cattle... and... beasts
a fitting competency for God's ministers
free of worldly business for the sanctuary

schools.summary: Gill: the dimensions were not left to the Israelites to give what they pleased, but fixed to a set length.
Clarke: the suburbs reach from the wall of the city and outward a thousand cubits round about.

themes: the suburbs... shall reach from the wall of the city and outward a thousand cubits
fixed by God, not the tribes' pleasure
the Levites' portion secured by a sure rule

schools.summary: Gill: beyond the first thousand, two thousand more were added on each side — the first for cattle and goods, the two thousand for gardens, orchards, fields, and vineyards; the city stood in the midst.

themes: ye shall measure from without the city... two thousand cubits... the city shall be in the midst
the fuller measure for fields and vineyards
God amply providing for his servants

schools.summary: Gill: the number of the cities is afterward expressed.
Wesley: the cities of refuge were assigned among the Levites, partly because they would be the most proper and impartial judges, and because their cities were dispersed through the land.

themes: there shall be six cities for refuge... that he may flee thither
the cities of mercy among God's ministers
Christ in whom priest and refuge meet

schools.summary: Gill: the names of these forty-eight cities, the tribes they were in, and which were cities of refuge, are given elsewhere (Josh. 21).
Keil & Delitzsch: all the cities given to the Levites were forty-eight, with their suburbs.

themes: all the cities which ye shall give to the Levites shall be forty and eight cities
the Levites dispersed through all the tribes
the scattering of Levi turned to a blessing (Josh. 21)

schools.summary: Gill: the cities came of what fell by lot to each tribe; they were not to refuse nor grudge to give them, the land being God's, they holding it under him.

themes: from them that have many ye shall give many; but from them that have few ye shall give few
the burden made fair by proportion
the land God's, the tribes his tenants

schools.summary: Gill: God continued his speech to Moses.
Henry: in the orders concerning the cities of refuge there is a great deal both of good law, in the case of murder and manslaughter, and of pure gospel.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the cities of refuge: good law and pure gospel
justice and mercy meeting, a type of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the word was now spoken directly to the children of Israel.
Keil & Delitzsch: when they were come over Jordan into Canaan, then they should appoint the cities of refuge.

themes: When ye be come over Jordan into the land of Canaan
the law of refuge for their settled life
God's forethought, mercy ordained before they entered

schools.summary: Gill: the Jewish writers say these were middling cities, set where there were markets, water, and people, with good roads kept to them; for the slayer who killed any person at unawares.

themes: ye shall appoint you cities to be cities of refuge... that the slayer may flee thither, which killeth any person at unawares
refuge for the unintentional slayer
mercy made easy of approach (Heb. 6:18)

schools.summary: Gill: the avenger was the near kinsman, to whom belonged the revenging of the blood of one killed, as the right of redemption belonged to such a one.
Clarke: the city was not a perpetual asylum but a refuge pro tempore, till the case could be heard.

themes: cities for refuge from the avenger; that the manslayer die not, until he stand before the congregation in judgment
spared from rash vengeance, brought to fair trial
the refuge a place of safety, not impunity

schools.summary: Gill: these six were of the forty-eight cities given to the Levites.
Keil & Delitzsch: six cities for refuge, three on each side of Jordan.

themes: of these cities which ye shall give six cities shall ye have for refuge
mercy with its appointed places
refuge near at hand for the fugitive

schools.summary: Gill: the three on the east were Bezer in Reuben, Ramoth in Gilead in Gad, and Golan in Bashan in Manasseh.
Ellicott: the Hebrew 'on this side' is fixed by the contrast with 'in the land of Canaan.'

themes: three cities on this side Jordan, and three cities... in the land of Canaan
refuge evenly divided for all the tribes
impartial mercy near to every one

schools.summary: Gill: the refuge was for the Israelite and the proselyte of righteousness alike, there being one law in things civil and religious.
Ellicott: 'stranger' (ger) properly denotes a foreigner dwelling among them.

themes: a refuge, both for the children of Israel, and for the stranger, and for the sojourner
one law sheltering native and foreigner alike
mercy without bound, open to Jew and Gentile (Eph. 2:13)

schools.summary: Gill: an instrument of iron such as a hatchet, hammer, sword, or knife. Calvin: God seems to contradict himself, absolving the involuntary slayer further on, yet here condemning whoever smites with iron; the weapon used betrays the intent.

themes: if he smite him with an instrument of iron, so that he die, he is a murderer
the deadly weapon betraying the intent
justice on the murderous will, not mischance

schools.summary: Gill: the Jews interpret this of a stone big enough to fill a man's hand, sufficient to kill.
Keil & Delitzsch: he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death.

themes: if he smite him with throwing a stone, wherewith he may die... he is a murderer
the deadly stone betraying the intent
the law judging the true purpose of the heart

schools.summary: Gill: a hand weapon of wood, such as a stick, staff, or club; if it could kill and the man died, the smiter is a murderer.

themes: if he smite him with an hand weapon of wood, wherewith he may die... he is a murderer
the deadly instrument the proof of intent
the sanctity of life guarded by sure punishment

schools.summary: Gill: the avenger not only had power but seemed obliged to slay the murderer, yet not before the case was heard and judged; the Targum adds 'in judgment,' when condemned by the court.

themes: The revenger of blood himself shall slay the murderer
the kinsman the appointed executioner
justice under the rule of judgment, not private revenge

schools.summary: Gill: this still speaks of one who shall die for murder — thrusting of hatred, or pushing him down from a high place; the malice marks the deed.

themes: if he thrust him of hatred, or hurl at him by laying of wait, that he die
the motive and manner proving murder
the heart's hatred the root of murder (1 John 3:15)

schools.summary: Gill: a blow with the fist on some vital part, issuing in death, given in enmity.
Keil & Delitzsch: he that smote him shall surely be put to death, for he is a murderer.

themes: in enmity smite him with his hand, that he die... he is a murderer
even a bare-handed blow in enmity is murder
the malice the guilt, the motive searched to its root

schools.summary: Gill: a sudden push, before one is aware, without any malicious design, but merely through accident, with no enmity and no lying in wait.

themes: if he thrust him suddenly without enmity, or have cast upon him any thing without laying of wait
the killing without malice is misadventure
God weighing the intent, sparing the unwilling hand

schools.summary: Gill: a stone sufficient to kill, but thrown or dropped without seeing the man; the slayer was not his enemy, neither sought his harm.

themes: with any stone... seeing him not... and was not his enemy, neither sought his harm
the marks of innocent mischance
the law trying each case by the heart

schools.summary: Gill: the congregation is the court of judicature, assembled to hear and try the cause, judging according to these statutes.

themes: the congregation shall judge between the slayer and the revenger of blood according to these judgments
the cause committed to lawful judgment
a fair trial guarding both innocent and avenger

schools.summary: Gill: the congregation put him under the care of proper persons to conduct him safely to the city, restraining the avenger.
Keil & Delitzsch: he abode there until the death of the high priest anointed with the holy oil.

themes: the congregation shall restore him to the city of his refuge... abide... unto the death of the high priest
the guilty freed by the high priest's death
the type of Christ our great High Priest (Heb. 9:12)

schools.summary: Gill: the border seems to be the suburbs assigned to the city; within it he was safe, but the slayer who left did so at his peril.

themes: if the slayer shall... come without the border of the city of his refuge
safety only within the refuge
abiding in Christ the place of security

schools.summary: Gill: this is if the avenger found him without the suburbs, fields, and vineyards belonging to the city.
Keil & Delitzsch: the avenger is guiltless in such a case.

themes: the revenger of blood... kill the slayer; he shall not be guilty of blood
the slayer forfeiting his safety by straying
the protection real but conditional

schools.summary: Gill: nothing but the high priest's death could give him liberty; the merciful provision yet carried some appearance of punishment, the man confined within the city's bounds, returning after that death to his possession.

themes: he should have remained... until the death of the high priest: but after the death of the high priest the slayer shall return
freed and restored by that death
the sinner restored by the death of Christ (Heb. 9:15)

schools.summary: Gill: this was a judicial law, according to which they were to proceed in all the above cases, throughout their generations in all their dwellings.

themes: these things shall be for a statute of judgment unto you throughout your generations
a perpetual standing rule
God's steady care for righteous judgment

schools.summary: Gill: this is of one who kills willingly, through enmity and malice.
Keil & Delitzsch: by the mouth of witnesses, but one witness shall not suffice to cause death.

themes: the murderer shall be put to death by the mouth of witnesses: but one witness shall not testify... to cause him to die
justice firm yet safe against false accusation
none to die but on certain proof (Deut. 19:15)

schools.summary: Gill: though he would give all his wealth and substance, no satisfaction was to be taken; the life of such a man was not to be saved by money, but he must surely die.

themes: ye shall take no satisfaction for the life of a murderer... but he shall be surely put to death
the absolute sanctity of human life
blood answered by blood (Gen. 9:6)

schools.summary: Gill: no satisfaction was to be taken even for him that fled for killing a man unawares.

Clarke: intended to typify that no sinner can be delivered from his banishment from God, or recover his forfeited inheritance, till Jesus Christ the great High Priest had died for his offences and risen again.

themes: ye shall take no satisfaction for him that is fled to... his refuge... until the death of the priest
no ransom for an early release
only the death of Christ frees the sinner

schools.summary: Gill: the land was not to be polluted, as it had been by the old inhabitants through idolatry, adultery, and murder.
Clarke: the land was considered guilty till the murderer's blood was shed in it; no wonder God is so strict against murderers.

themes: blood it defileth the land: and the land cannot be cleansed... but by the blood of him that shed it
the gravity of bloodshed, answered only by blood
the deeper defilement cleansed in Christ (Heb. 9:22)

schools.summary: Gill: the exhortation is strengthened by this — the Holy God dwells there, as in the tabernacle erected among them; the defiling of the land was the more grievous because of his presence.

themes: Defile not therefore the land which ye shall inherit, wherein I dwell: for I the LORD dwell among the children of Israel
God's presence the ground of holiness
those among whom God dwells to keep clean (2 Cor. 6:16)
